answer:
For Charles Dunoyer and his close associate Charles Comte—two leading lights of early 19th-century French liberalism (often termed the "industrialist" school alongside figures like Jean-Baptiste Say)—"industrial" (*industriel*) and "industry" (*industrie*) denote a broad mode of human activity and social organization defined by the peaceful, skillful creation of utility and exchangeable value (*valeur*), primarily by acting upon external things (*choses*) rather than coercing or despoiling fellow humans.[^1] This contrasts sharply with prior "regimes" of violence: savage hunting, nomadic plunder, slavery, feudal serfdom, or privilege/monopoly.[^2]

### Etymology and Core Meaning
Dunoyer traces *industrie* to Latin *industria* (from *intus-struere*: "to construct within," implying intelligent action on/in things toward an end). It originally signified any skillful action (*art*, *adresse*), moral or immoral (e.g., an "honest industry" vs. a swindler's "knights of industry"). Over time, as political economy advanced, it narrowed to **useful, productive labor** that generates wealth (utilities/values) without harming others—distinguished from *spoliation* (theft, extortion, conquest).[^3]

- **Industrial action**: Applying faculties (*facultés*) to modify things (e.g., agriculture, manufacturing) *or persons* (e.g., teaching, medicine, governance as moral formation), yielding durable, exchangeable results. Labor itself vanishes upon execution, but its utility accumulates (e.g., a teacher's lesson fixes knowledge in minds; a doctor's care restores health).[^4]
- **Non-industrial**: Any violent or parasitic gain (e.g., war, privilege, crime—even if "skillful"). Crime destroys value; only moral industry creates it.[^5]
- **Industrial society** (*société industrielle*) or **regime** (*régime industriel*): A state where *all* subsist via such labor/exchange, sans privileges, slavery, or conquest. Government becomes a "commercial company" for public order (repressing violence), funded voluntarily.[^6] All professions qualify if productive: plowman to statesman.[^7]

Comte echoes this in framing liberty/property as flowing from free appropriation (non-violent industry), critiquing slavery-based jurisprudence; he references Dunoyer's usage approvingly.[^8]

### Key Traits
- **Universal scope**: Encompasses *all* utility-creating arts—material (farming, commerce) and immaterial (sciences, arts, morals). Rejects Adam Smith's narrow view (only material labor "productive").[^9]
- **Moral dimension**: Paired with morality (*moralité*); industry fosters temperance, as gain requires effort/prudence.[^10] Enables maximum liberty: full faculty exercise without harming self/others.[^11]
- **Inequalities persist**: Natural differences (talent, effort) yield unequal outcomes (wealth, freedom), even sans violence—beneficial for emulation/progress.[^12]
- **Peaceful rivalry**: Competition stimulates without oppression; associations build (e.g., canals, insurance), unlike privilege-corporations.[^13]

### Difference from Later Senses
Post-1830s (e.g., Saint-Simonism, Marxism, modern usage), *industriel* narrowed to **manufacturing/capitalist production** (factories, machines, proletariat vs. bourgeoisie), often connoting exploitation, class war, or mechanized drudgery. Dunoyer/Comte's sense is **pre-capitalist, anti-exploitative**:
| Aspect | 19th-C. Liberal (*Industrie*) | Later/Modern (*Industrial*) |
|--------|-------------------------------|-----------------------------|
| **Scope** | All peaceful value-creation (things *or* persons: ethics, education, governance). | Factories/manufacturing; excludes "services"/immaterial. |
| **Class** | No fixed classes; fluid via merit. All "proletarians" if productive. | Proletariat vs. capitalists; inherent antagonism. |
| **Morality** | Inherently moral/peaceful; violence = non-industrial. | Often amoral/exploitative; "industrial revolution" = dehumanizing. |
| **Society** | Liberty-maximizing; dissolves nations into peaceful "municipal" clusters. | State-managed capitalism/socialism; mass production/society. |

Their *industrie* is classical-liberal "catallactics" (peaceful exchange) avant la lettre—optimistic, universalist, anti-statist—vs. later materialist/collectivist framings.[^14]

[^1]: Charles Dunoyer, *Nouveau traité d'économie*, Vol. I, Ch. 11, [p-cd2259](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/11-i-liberte-compatible-avec-la-maniere-de-vivre-des-/translation/#p-cd2259).

[^2]: Ibid., Introduction [p-67acca](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/00-introduction/translation/#p-67acca); Ch. 11 [p-f7bdb7](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/11-i-liberte-compatible-avec-la-maniere-de-vivre-des-/translation/#p-f7bdb7).

[^3]: Ibid., Ch. 11 [p-06038f](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/11-i-liberte-compatible-avec-la-maniere-de-vivre-des-/translation/#p-06038f) to [p-4329f5](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/11-i-liberte-compatible-avec-la-maniere-de-vivre-des-/translation/#p-4329f5).

[^4]: *Nouveau traité d'économie*, Vol. II, Ch. 13 [p-62915e](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-ii-d6ae03/13-iii-des-divers-ordres-de-travaux-et-de-fonctions-q/translation/#p-62915e) to [p-76ee09](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-ii-d6ae03/13-iii-des-divers-ordres-de-travaux-et-de-fonctions-q/translation/#p-76ee09).

[^5]: Ibid., Vol. I, Ch. 11 [p-314852](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/11-i-liberte-compatible-avec-la-maniere-de-vivre-des-/translation/#p-314852); Vol. II, Ch. 13 [p-fb8d6b](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-ii-d6ae03/13-iii-des-divers-ordres-de-travaux-et-de-fonctions-q/translation/#p-fb8d6b).

[^6]: Vol. I, Ch. 11 [p-872da7](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/11-i-liberte-compatible-avec-la-maniere-de-vivre-des-/translation/#p-872da7).

[^7]: Vol. II, Ch. 13 [p-50babc](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-ii-d6ae03/13-iii-des-divers-ordres-de-travaux-et-de-fonctions-q/translation/#p-50babc).

[^8]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété*, Vol. I, Ch. 3 [p-2ffb13](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/03-de-ce-qui-constitue-la-liberte/translation/#p-2ffb13).

[^9]: Dunoyer, *Nouveau traité*, Vol. II, Ch. 13 [p-c1b997](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-ii-d6ae03/13-iii-des-divers-ordres-de-travaux-et-de-fonctions-q/translation/#p-c1b997).

[^10]: Vol. I, Ch. 11 [p-b990e3](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/11-i-liberte-compatible-avec-la-maniere-de-vivre-des-/translation/#p-b990e3).

[^11]: Ibid., Introduction [p-d37334](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/00-introduction/translation/#p-d37334).

[^12]: Vol. I, Ch. 12 (full chapter: [/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/12-ii-obstacles-qui-sopposent-encore-a-la-liberte-dan/translation/]).

[^13]: Ch. 11 [p-40e4c5](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/11-i-liberte-compatible-avec-la-maniere-de-vivre-des-/translation/#p-40e4c5).

[^14]: Corpus lacks later contrasts; distinction from historical context/search patterns.
